title: Victoria sponge
subtitle: The classic British layer cake with jam and cream
excerpt: Two 20 cm golden sponge layers made by the equal-weight creaming method, sandwiched with raspberry jam and lightly whipped cream. The foundational British cake. Serves eight.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the Victoria sandwich is recorded in the cake chapter as a plain sponge made by the creaming method, named for Queen Victoria and filled with jam. The equal-weight ratio of butter, sugar, flour, and eggs (1:1:1:1 by weight) is documented as the standard British sandwich cake proportion. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: a creamed sponge cake using beaten eggs and butter is documented as the foundational British layer cake from which the Victoria sandwich derives.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled, cooled sponge layers individually, wrapped in cling film, for up to 2 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the layers a day ahead; cool, wrap, and store at room temperature. Fill and dust with icing sugar on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; the sponge is done when it springs back to a gentle press and a skewer inserted through the centre comes out clean

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake-making technique in which butter and sugar are beaten together until pale and fluffy before the eggs and flour are added. Beating aerates the butter, creating tiny air bubbles that expand during baking to produce a light, even crumb. The mixture should be noticeably pale and increased in volume — about 3 minutes with an electric mixer or 5 minutes by hand. Under-creaming produces a dense, greasy sponge; over-creaming after the flour is added collapses the air bubbles and toughens the crumb.

content.text: A Victoria sponge is built on the

and the equal-weight ratio: the same weight of butter, sugar, flour, and eggs. Weigh the eggs in their shells first and match the other ingredients exactly to that weight. Two 20 cm sandwich tins, 5 cm deep, take the batter from four medium eggs.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease and line the bases of two 20 cm sandwich tins. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale, light, and noticeably increased in volume; about 3 minutes with an electric mixer. Add the {{eggs}} one at a time, beating well after each. If the mixture curdles, add a tablespoon of the flour and beat again. Fold in the sifted {{self-raising-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} with a large metal spoon or spatula, cutting through in a figure-of-eight motion until just combined. Divide equally between the two tins; a set of scales ensures even layers.

Bake for 22–25 minutes until the sponges have risen to the edge of the tin, are deep gold on top, and spring back when pressed lightly in the centre. A skewer inserted through the middle should come out clean. Cool in the tins for 5 minutes before turning out onto a rack.

Whip the {{whipping-cream}} to soft peaks; it should hold its shape when the beaters are lifted but not be stiff. Spread the {{raspberry-jam}} on the flat side of the bottom layer, then spread the whipped cream on top of the jam. Place the second sponge on top. Dust with {{icing-sugar}} before serving.
